symptom: Azure Virtual Desktop (AVD) virtual machines (VMs) are experiencing power state issues and health status showing as "Unavailable."
rootCause: The problem can arise due to several reasons: Power State Issues: The VM might be turned off or not properly configured to stay in the "Running" state.  Health Status Unavailable: The AVD session host might not pass critical health checks, such as domain join checks or side-by-side stack listener health checks
solution: Verify VM Power State:                Ensure the VM is turned on and configured to stay in the "Running" state. You can use the Azure portal or Azure CLI to check and manage the VM's power state.         2. Check Health Status:                Use the Azure portal to review the health status of the AVD session hosts. Identify any failed health checks and follow the recommended steps to resolve them                        3. Configure Alerts:               Set up alerts in Azure Monitor to proactively notify you of any power state or health status issues. Use the following query to monitor both VM power state and session host health status: --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- union (     arg('').resources     | where type == "microsoft.compute/virtualmachines"     | extend machineStatus = properties.extended.instanceView.powerState.displayStatus ), (     arg("").desktopvirtualizationresources     | where type == "microsoft.desktopvirtualization/hostpools/sessionhosts"     | where properties.['status'] == "Unavailable"     | extend agentstatus = properties.status     | project name, agentstatus ) | project name, agentstatus  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------             4. Restart Remote Desktop Services:                  Restart the Remote Desktop Services on the affected VMs. This can help resolve connectivity issues and ensure the VMs are properly configured           5. Update Remote Desktop Client:                    Ensure you have the latest version of the Remote Desktop client installed. Sometimes, updating the client can resolve connectivity issues  ================================================================= Steps to Create an Alert Using the Above Query Access Azure Monitor:  Go to the Azure portal and navigate to Azure Monitor. Create a New Alert Rule:  Select Alerts from the Azure Monitor menu. Click on New alert rule. Define Scope:  Set the scope to the resource you want to monitor (e.g., your AVD VMs). Add Condition:  In the Condition tab, select Custom log search. Paste the above Kusto query into the query editor. Configure Alert Logic:  Define the logic for the alert. For example, you can set the alert to trigger when the query returns results indicating a VM is not in the "Running" state or a session host is "Unavailable". Set Up Action Group:  Create or select an existing action group to define how you want to be notified (e.g., email, SMS, webhook). Review and Create:  Review the alert rule settings and click Create alert rule.  By following these steps, you can effectively monitor and receive alerts for power state and health status issues in your Azure Virtual Desktop environment.

symptom: Users attempting to connect to an Azure Virtual Desktop (AVD) session are encountering an orchestration failure. The error originates from the orchistration process when attempting to add the user to the Remote Desktop Users group.Error Details - Error Source: RDAgent - Error Internal: False - Error Operation: AddUserToRDUGroup - Error Code: -2147467259 - Symbolic Error Code: ConnectionFailedAdTrustedRelationshipFailure - Error Message: - Exception of type 'Microsoft.RDInfra.RDAgent.Service.AddUserToLocalGroupAdTrustedRelationshipFailureException' was thrown. Configuration & Observations- The host pool is configured as a personal host pool with direct assignment. - Diagnostic activity logs indicate users are connecting to multiple session hosts.User Identity Validation:- Checked whether the user is an Entra ID user or an AD-created user synced to Entra ID.  Customer's Deployment Setup- DevOps Pipeline: Uses Terraform to deploy AVD environments.- Personal Host Pool: - New VMs are created daily as part of Privileged Access Workstation (PAW) machines using the DevOps pipeline.- Users access session hosts via the PAW application, which triggers a PowerShell script to assign the session host.
rootCause: Issue Analysis- Logs indicate failure in adding users to the Remote Desktop Users group when done via automation.- Manual assignment using the Azure Portal works flawlessly: - User logs off, removes the previous assignment, and assigns a new session host via the portal. - The user is successfully added to the Remote Desktop Users group, and connections work. - Key difference: Assignment done via PowerShell script (PAW automation) fails, while Azure Portal assignment succeeds.  Potential Root Causes- PowerShell Script or DevOps Pipeline Issue- The script may not have the required permissions or fail due to an Entra ID/AD trust issue.- Possible execution context problems�check whether the script runs under the correct identity. - Missing Group Membership Validation - Ensure the script correctly adds users to the Remote Desktop Users group with proper permissions. - Entra ID & AD Synchronization Delays - If the user is created in AD and synced to Entra ID, there might be a propagation delay causing failures.
solution: Recommended Fixes1.Validate PowerShell Script ExecutionRun the script manually on an affected session host:$hostPoolName = "YourHostPool"$resourceGroupName = "YourResourceGroup" $vmName = "Your-VM-Name" $aadUserPrincipalName = "user@domain.com"  New-AzWvdUserSessionHost `     -HostPoolName $hostPoolName `     -ResourceGroupName $resourceGroupName `     -SessionHostName "$vmName.yourdomain.com" `     -UserPrincipalName $aadUserPrincipalNameThis not under scope of Microsoft CSS if the customer is using Devops pileline to perform the action. 2. Check Group Membership ManuallyOn the affected session host, run:Get-LocalGroupMember -Group "Remote Desktop Users"- Ensure the user appears in the list. - If missing, try adding them manually:   Add-LocalGroupMember -Group "Remote Desktop Users" -Member "user@domain.com" - If manual addition works but script fails, review role-based access permissions. 3. Audit Entra ID & AD Sync- Check Azure AD Connect sync logs for delays/errors. - Run:    Get-MsolUser -UserPrincipalName "user@domain.com" - Ensure group memberships are updated in Entra ID.  The issue is likely rooted in PowerShell-based automation (PAW assignment) vs. Azure Portal manual assignment. Customer should check their powershell script that assgines user to session host.

symptom: You have an Windows AVD setup following these steps: https://dev.azure.com/Supportability/WindowsVirtualDesktop/_wiki/wikis/WindowsVirtualDesktop/1198769/Build-a-lab?anchor=step-4%3A-deploy-your-first-avd-host-pool-using-the-azure-portal We have a Domain controller and some hosts in Active Directory.  Then have MFA setup and SSO.  Have enablerdsaadauth:i:1 setting set in your AVD hostpool "rdp properties".  When connecting with a standard user using AAD authentication, all is working fine (you have the SSO experience).  When connecting with an administrative account, you get endlessly prompted for your AAD credentials (with authenticator validation succeeding). The sign-in is seen as "successful" in Entra. But you are prompted again.
rootCause: Administrative accounts are part of the protected groups (Administrators, Domain Admins, Domain Controllers, Backup Operators, Server Operators, Account Operators, Cert Publishers, Enterprise Admins, and Schema Admins).  By design admins (high priviledge accounts) cannot sign in to on prem resources as stated in :  FAQs for hybrid FIDO2 security key deployment - Microsoft Entra ID | Microsoft Learn "The default security policy doesn't grant Microsoft Entra permission to sign high privilege accounts on to on-premises resources. Due to possible attack vectors from Microsoft Entra ID to Active Directory, it's not recommended to unblock these accounts by relaxing the Password Replication Policy of the computer object CN=AzureADKerberos,OU=Domain Controllers,<domain-DN>."
solution: Workaround 1: Do not set the enablerdsaadauth:i:1 setting in the rdp properties of the hostpool. This way, you will connect using Windows authentication and this will succeed even for an admin account.   Workaround 2: Remove the user from the Protected Groups.   Workaround 3 (just for test or to validate this is this issue): The default security policy doesn't grant Azure AD permission to sign high privilege accounts on to on-premises resources. To unblock the accounts, use Active Directory Users and Computers to modify the msDS-NeverRevealGroup property of the Azure AD Kerberos Computer object (CN=AzureADKerberos,OU=Domain Controllers,) on the domain controller machine:       After removal all works fine. But from a security point of view this should not be done. High priviledge accounts should not be removed from the msDS-NeverRevealGroup attribute.

symptom: After updating a Windows App, the following error prevents it from launching:Error message The service cannot be started, either because it is disabled or because it has no enabled devices associated with it.  The issue occurs for all users and persists even after reinstalling the app.
rootCause: This issue occurs when the Microsoft Account Sign-in Assistant service (`wlidsvc`) is set to [Disabled] as its startup type.
solution: Follow these steps to resolve the issue by setting the startup type of the service back to [Manual]: Sign in to the affected environment with a user account that has administrator privileges. Open the Services management tool by typing services.msc in the search bar and pressing Enter.Locate the Microsoft Account Sign-in Assistant service in the list and double-click it to open its properties.In the General tab, change the Startup type to [Manual].Click OK to apply the changes.Restart the operating system as a precaution.After restarting, try launching the Windows App and verify if the issue is resolved.

symptom:
After upgrading an Azure VM from Windows 10 to Windows 11 multi-session OS, the ability to mount ISO files via right-click in File Explorer is no longer available.   This is due to a design change in Windows 11. On Windows 10 multi-session OS, standard users could right-click an ISO file in File Explorer and select Mount. On Windows 11 multi-session OS, the same action prompts for User Account Control (UAC), and standard users cannot mount ISO files directly.     This change affects Windows Server OS with RDSH role on Windows Server 2022 or later. The normal user will see below result with configurations:   Normal User can mount .iso file in the following condition is met.

  Cannot mount the .iso file
  and a UAC prompt appears.
solution: On Windows 11 multi-session OS and Server OS with RDSH role, ISO mounting by standard users cannot be enabled through registry settings. UAC is mandatory.Workaround: Perform the mount operation using File Explorer with administrative privileges.

symptom: Abstract Several screen issues happen on the desktop on AVD (Azure Virtual Desktop) or Windows 365. Major Symptoms The mouse cursor doesn't show. The invalid white vertical lines show on the screen. The screen or mouse cursor is flicking.
rootCause: In many cases, the issue is with the display driver.However, sometimes the cause lies with the MPO (multiplane overlay). In this case, disabling the MPO might solve the issue. MPO Support - Windows drivers | Microsoft Learn
solution: Firstly, you should confirm whether the display driver is the latest version on the client machine on the client machine that is the device you are connecting from.If the display driver is *not* the latest version, please update to the latest version. If your issue isn't still fixed even though the latest version of the display driver, please set the registry below follow these steps on the client machine on the client machine that is the device you are connecting from. *Don't set the registry on the AVD or Windows 365 virtual machine. Open the registry editor (regedit.exe) Moe to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\Dwm Click [Edit] menu. Select [New] -> [DWORD (32-bit) Value] Rename the value name from [New Value #1] to [OverlayTestMode] Double-Click [OverlayTestMode] Set the value data to "5" Push [OK] button Close the registry editor (regedit.exe) Reboot the client machine.

symptom: Introduction of AVD Diag Tool     AVD Diag Tool serves as a centralized Dashboard for examining AVD problems.      You can access the AVD Diag from https://aka.ms/avddiagtool   *If you do not have permission, please contact someone with Editor access to grant you the necessary permissions. *We would like to understand the number of users of this tool quantitatively in order to assess its impact. By accessing the tool via the shortlink we created above, we can count the number of accesses. Therefore, when using AVD Diag, please bookmark the shortlink and access the tool through it.      Training Video   The link below offers detailed instructions on how to use the AVD Diag Tool.  The below recording video shows AVD Diag tool overview and how to use with its demo. Introduction of AVD Diag  A Tool for AVD Investigation-20250808_002100UTC-Meeting Recording.mp4   Tutorial for Beginner     The following site allows you to experience the investigation process using the AVD Diag Tool by utilizing a tutorial prepared on the actual AVD Diag Tool, which guides you step by step through the operation checks.  AVD Diagnostics   Description   The following explains each feature displayed under "Activity Diagnostics" when launching the AVD Diag Tool.    Activity - Activities are displayed based on the information entered at the top of the page. You can refer to the displayed data and adjust the input at the top of the page if necessary.     Diag Error - Displays logs marked as errors within the Kusto Diag Activity.   Chart of ErrorCodeSymbolic - Shows a list of ErrorCodeSymbolic related to the specified information you filtered.   Error Internal Rate  - Displays the percentage of errors that include Internal Error among the shown errors.   RDP Shortpath or NOT - Indicates whether the connection is using RDP Shortpath.   Infra Trace - Displays information recorded in the Kusto RD Infra Trace table.   Client Trace - Displays information recorded in the Kusto RDClientTrace table.   Session Host Trace - Displays information recorded in the RDPCoreTSEventLog table, related to the Session Host.   Agent to Broker Heartbeat - Displays heartbeat information to confirm connectivity between the Session Host and the control plane.   FSLogix Trace - Displays information recorded in the RDFSLogixTrace table.   HostPool Info - Displays information related to the specified AVD environment and Host Pool.   Session Host Info - Displays information about the specified Session Host (ShoeboxAgentHealth data).   VM Operations - Displays operations performed on the specified Session Host. (Same as VM Operations shown in ASC.)  Deploy Error - Displays VM deployment errors for a specific Session Host.  Logon Delay - Shows the time taken to complete logon, broken down by each component.   Agent Update Failure Check - Displays information about failed agent updates.   TooManyRequests (429) - Displays the number of failed TokenRequest API calls due to TooManyRequests based on specified conditions. (If this spikes, it may indicate that the number of requests to VMs within a certain time frame has hit a limit.) Customer has Azure monitor
for AVD set up and has LA queries configured to send email alerts as soon as
the VM goes unavailable. However customer has been
receiving false/positive alters post the configuration. Alert email has been
sent stating that the VM has been unavailable but the status of the actual VM
in AVD hostpool was always available.  There were no connection
issues/impacts reported during the mentioned time. There are no service restarts
or any other alarming errors on the VM event logs on the specified time. Kusto queries also confirm
that the VM has been available. It is confirmed that the VMs
status was available but LA is reporting the VM as unavailable.
rootCause: Expected behavior.
solution:
Resolution :   
 It is
     expected for AVD agent to disconnect & re-connect to the back AVD
     service momentarily for few secs. This is
     by-design and does not impact the production and moreover the VM would
     still show as available. However
     LA triggers an alert for this expected disconnect/re-connect.   
 
    To
avoid this, PG has suggested to change the LA query in way that it only alerts
VMs that have been in unavailable state for over 2mins. This would eliminate
the expected false positives alerts & provide an alert only when the VM is
really impacted & unavailable.

symptom: Users local Microsoft Teams calls disconnect shortly (within ~1020 seconds) after they launch a Remote VDI session. The problem is reproducible when the Teams account signed in locally differs from the Teams account inside the VDI. At the moment the VDI session starts, Teams in VDI initializes/optimizes media, and the local call drops.
rootCause: Competing Teams media stacks (local machine vs. VDIoptimized Teams) contend for HID/audio devices and WebRTC/optimization channels.  The account/tenant mismatch between local Teams and VDI Teams increases the chance that VDI media optimization or device redirection seizes control, causing the local call session to tear down.
solution:
Resolution 1  Disable Teams HID Manager (low risk, reversible) Mitigates device conflict by stopping Teams from actively managing HID peripherals when VDI starts. Registry (peruser): Path:  HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Teams\HID
Name:  DisableHidManagerV1
Type:  REG_DWORD
Value: 1
PowerShell (user context): PowerShell      New-Item -Path "HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Teams\HID" -Force | Out-Null New-ItemProperty -Path "HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Teams\HID" -Name "DisableHidManagerV1" -PropertyType DWord -Value 1 -Force | Out-Null                Deployment tip: Roll out via Intune (user script) or GPO (User Configuration  Preferences  Registry). Pilot first, then expand. Resolution 2  Prevent or Delay Teams AutoStart Inside VDI Avoids the collision during an active local call by not starting Teams in the VDI session immediately. Remove autostart entries (user context in VDI): PowerShell      $runKey = "HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run"  # Classic Teams if (Test-Path "$runKey\com.squirrel.Teams.Teams") {   Remove-ItemProperty -Path $runKey -Name "com.squirrel.Teams.Teams" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue }  # New Teams if (Test-Path "$runKey\com.microsoft.teams") {   Remove-ItemProperty -Path $runKey -Name "com.microsoft.teams" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue }                 Set client preference Open Teams automatically = Off in the base profile or enforce via script/GPO. User guidance: Start/finish calls in one environment; avoid launching VDI midcall or launch VDI first and use the same environment for the call.  Validation & Rollout Pilot with a small user group:
Group A: Resolution 1 only. Group B: Resolution 1 + 2.   Test scenario: Start a local Teams call  launch VDI  observe for 2 minutes. Success criteria: The local call remains connected; no unexpected device flip or session drop. Scale: Use Intune/GPO for registry/policy deployment; bake service changes into the VDI gold image where applicable.

symptom: We have located an issue where Microsoft Runtime application dependency may become corrupt when 2 or more users log onto a Session Host and use that app. The issue occurs when a user logs off and breaks the Runtime so on next user logon, the app may fail Here is the scenario:  User A connects to AVD Session host launches 2 apps  ( app attach ( VHDX) that depend on Runtime 1.4 ( pre installed ) User B connects to AVD Session Host launches the same 2 apps that User A has launched User B closes the apps and logs off User A closes the app and logs off User B logs back on and now get the 2 apps greyed out on the start menu, and gets the following on App Launch       Check the Runtime  and its broken:      This is happening on 24H2 ( at the time of writing 23H2 and 25H2 have not been tested)
rootCause: During logoff, app attach in the RDAgent calls PackageManager.RemovePackageAsync() to deregister packages for the logged off user. This triggers automatic removal of framework dependencies. The removal operation enters "user profile changing" state while Windows/Appx is unloading the user profile.   When the profile becomes inaccessible mid-operation (because logoff could be complete), Appx fails to complete the removal and attempts to roll back. The rollback fails because it tries to restore state to the now-unloaded user profile, causing Appx to mark the framework package as "Modified, NeedsRemediation.  To summarize, it's a race condition between profile offloading during logoff and package deregistration.
solution:
Workarounds:   Block logoff until removal of package is done (impacts user experience) Stage the remediated package immediately if it goes into a bad state Instead of registering dependency package by full name, register package by family name. This would ensure Appx doesn't deregister/removes the dependency package upon logoff that causes this issue.    The other option for the customer is for the user to remove the dependency from their package manifest, but that requires repackaging and updating the package.  Preferred workaround:  There are 2 scripts available.   Create-FrameworkStagingTask.ps1
-  You can test this manually on a session host to ensure all is working. Run the script via Powershell ISE in elevated context. It will prompt you for the Runtime Location source, e.g c:\temp\Micrsoft.WindowsAppRuntime.msix. Enter the full path inc the file name, Once complete you will see    Check in Task Scheduler , the newly created Task appears:    Once you are happy , you can now deploy that
script via the other PowerShell script Deploy-FrameworkStagingTask-AzureVMs.ps1
to all session hosts in the HP:   You can do it via Azure CLI 
PowerShell   It will prompt you for the Resource
Group containing the Session Hosts   And then it will ask you for the
location of the runtime dependency which will be needed.       In my test I just copied the
Microsoft.WindowsAppRuntime to c:\temp\ on each of my 2 session hosts in a Temp
folder and specified that location ( as you can see above) or provide a share location containing the RunTime.  Check each session host that the scheduled task is created:      Please reach out to the content owner to access the scripts.

symptom: Unexpected hostname changes occurred on AVD session hosts at the operating system level, while the Azure VM resource names remained unchanged.    This caused confusion in the AVD host pool because the new names appeared there, even though the underlying VM names were intact. The behavior was not triggered by the AVD platform but likely by an Intune/device policy or manual user action inside the OS.
rootCause: Rename occurred inside the OS (not by AVD). Evidence includes NetSetup.log (NetpChangeMachineName), Event 1074 (planned shutdown), Event 6011 (name change), and Security Event 4688 indicating SystemPropertiesComputerName.exe was launched
solution: Verified that AVD service was not responsible for the rename. Provided detailed analysis and guidance to review Intune or domain policies

symptom: When typing in Japanese in the Windows App on ChromeOS, two prediction or input suggestion windows are displayed simultaneously. This can cause confusion and make it difficult to select the correct input suggestion.
rootCause: Duplicate predictive input sources This issue occurs because both the source ChromeOS device and the destination AVD session host virtual machine or Windows 365 Cloud PC are providing predictive input. Each environment is running its own input method editor (IME) with predictive input enabled, which results in duplicate suggestion windows.
solution: Configure input settings to use only Microsoft IME on the destination To resolve the issue, configure your environment so that only the Microsoft IME predictive input on the destination AVD session host virtual machine or Windows 365 Cloud PC is used. Follow these steps: On Windows App: Go to General settings. Under Use scan code input when available, turn the toggle On.   On ChromeOS: Open Keyboard settings. Set the keyboard language to US.   On AVD or Windows 365 Cloud PC: Set the keyboard layout to Japanese.    Note The keyboard layout will operate as a US keyboard layout.

symptom:
Sxs not getting auto installed post registering the machine to hostpool this makes the machine not showing up on the hostpool -- session host blade   Application event log shows following error. Unable to retrieve DefaultAgent from registry: System.NullReferenceException: Object reference not set to an instance of an object  Collected MSI logs with procmon while installing the agents.   Identified that the loading module for RDagentbootloader was from Syswow64 which is for 32bit.  As per the internal bug if the RDagent bootloader uses 32bit module it will fail to query the registry to set the values  Bug 44929841
[AVD] RDAgentBootLoader.exe cannot open registry _bootLoaderRegPath if it is
running as 32-bit process
rootCause: The following registry was causing the issue.  HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\.NETFramework ---- Enable64bit was set to 0   This forces RDagentbootloader to load 32bit module and that was not allowing the agentbootloader to query the registry and fails with "Unable to retrieve default agent from registry"
solution:
On
     the affected machine navigate to following registry path. 
 HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\.NETFramework 
 Change
     Enable64bit value from 0 to 1.

symptom:
Customers are getting protocol error
 (code: 0x112F) due to which the remote session will be disconnected. And error
 message displayed is Please try connecting to the remote computer again.
rootCause:
This is happening only when customer is
 using Nvidia GPU enabled VMs and enable multi-monitor features from RDP
 properties. This is an AVC hardware issue. AVC HW
 is enabled in the session hosts which has Nvidia GPU enabled for which
 multi-monitor is not working and throwing the error protocol error (code:
 0x112f) due to which the remote session will be disconnected.
solution:
We need to Disable the AVC HW in the
 session hosts which has Nvidia GPU enabled for things to work in multi-monitor
 environments as well. 
 Mitigation steps to disable is: 
 Launch "Group Policy Editor"

 implement the same via GPO in case of multiple session hosts got affected. Drill down to the policies: Computer

 Desktop Services> Remote Desktop Session Host> Remote Session
 Environment Disable the following policy:
 Configure H.264/AVC hardware encoding for Remote Desktop connections.   
 
 
 This needs to be done in all the session
 hosts which has Nvidia GPU enabled if done locally for testing. If successful,
 ask to implement the same through GPO.

symptom: Abstract  An issue was observed in which copy-and-paste functionality fails when using the Windows App (Web) in full-screen mode on macOS with the Chrome browser to access Azure Virtual Desktop (AVD).While content copied on the local macOS client cannot be pasted into the AVD session in full-screen mode, the issue does not occur in windowed mode, and paste functionality continues to work after returning to full-screen once a paste has been performed. This behavior is unintended and was confirmed by the development team. Workaround is to Disabe [Keyboard shortcuts] setting this option at Windows App version  2.0.03267.1373. Further investigation is ongoing to determine whether the issue can be fully resolved with keyboard shortcuts enabled. Additional details are tracked in the related ICM incident.  Symptoms **Steps to Reproduce:** 1.  On macOS, open Chrome browser and connect to Windows App  2.  Connect to a desktop in the AVD environment. 3.  Switch to full-screen mode. 4.  Copy content on macOS (the client machine). 5.  Attempt to paste into the AVD environment in full-screen mode (step 3). 6.  Confirm that paste does not work.              **Shortcut key used:**  (Command) + V.       Right-click paste also reproduces the issue.            The following behavior was observed: *   **Full Screen:** Right-click paste and shortcut paste are not possible. *   **Window Mode:** Right-click paste (_) and shortcut paste are possible._       _(_) Until the shortcut paste is performed, right-click paste cannot be executed.
solution:
In Windows App (Web) version 2.0.03267.1373, disabling the Keyboard shortcuts option in the settings enables copyandpaste functionality and serves as an effective workaround for this issue. Red marked setting in attached image is it. Whether this issue can be resolved while keeping Keyboard shortcuts enabled is currently under investigation by the development team as of January 26, 2026.
For further details, please refer to the attached ICM.

symptom: Non persistent VDI environment accessing internet based remote management consoles which has a virtual keyboard and the virtual keyboard works well on the Windows 10 AVD non persistent VDI but virtual keyboard doesn't work on the Windows 11 AVD VDI host.    Customer was using RDP method for connecting to AVD VDI.
rootCause: Actual cause was not determined but appears to be problem with using RDP method for connection having problem recognizing keystrokes as opposed to a cloud-based method for connection to AVD desktop which was then connecting to web-based 3rd party management consoles. Hypothesis is it just needed a cloud-based method for connecting to AVD in order for the keystrokes to be recognized with an internet-based management console.
solution: Utilized Windows App to connect to AVD VDI which resolved recognizing keystrokes with internet-based management type consoles.

symptom: The session hosts appear in a 'Shutdown' state under hostpool blade, even though the virtual machine is running:  When users attempt to restart the VM or perform a stop/start operation, the session host transitions to a 'Needs Assistance' state and reports that the required URLs are not reachable.
rootCause: After reviewing the VNET configuration, it was identified that VNET encryption was enabled, which also has an ExpressRoute gateway attached.  This combination is a known limitation and is not supported due to its instability and it may cause connectivity issues.  What is Azure Virtual Network encryption? - Azure Virtual Network | Microsoft Learn
solution: Once VNet encryption was disabled, the session host was able to successfully connect to all required endpoints without reporting any further issues.

symptom: When you connect through the Windows App web client to an AVD session host, Windows 365 Cloud PC, or Dev Box, and launch PowerShell in the legacy Windows Console Host, you can only input the Enter key, while all other character inputs (regardless of language or type) are ignored. This occurs regardless of whether PowerShell is launched with standard or administrator permissions. The issue occurs only in these scenarios: Connection is established through the Windows App web client. PowerShell is launched in the Windows Console Host.  The issue doesn't occur when: Connecting via the Windows App (desktop version). Using cmd.exe (Command Prompt). Launching PowerShell in Windows Terminal.  This means the issue can be easily avoided by using Windows Terminal instead of Windows Console Host, or by using the Windows App instead of the web client. Windows Console Host (issue occurs)Windows Terminal (issue doesn't occur)
rootCause: According to the product groups formal statement, this behavior is caused by a limitation of the Windows App web client and is deemed by design. Because the issue can be avoided by connecting through the desktop version of Windows App or by using Windows Terminal, related IcMs were resolved with this explanation.  If the customer has sufficient BI by which they strongly require to use PowerShell launched via the legacy Windows Console Host specifically through the web client, you are advised to submit a DesignChange Request (DCR).DCR or Feature Request - Overview
solution: Resolution 1: Connect using the Windows App desktop version Instead of connecting through the Windows App web client, use the Windows App desktop version. Resolution 2: Use Windows Terminal to run PowerShell Launch PowerShell in Windows Terminal rather than in the Windows Console Host. On Windows 11, new PowerShell prompt are launched in Windows Terminal for standard users and in Windows Console Host for users with administrative privileged. You can configure the default terminal application for both instances by going to Settings > System > Advanced settings.

rootCause: This issue happens due to a code defect in the UDP transport handler of the Windows App. The issue is currently being investigated in the following bug:  Bug 60104911 Memory Leak in rdpnanoTransport!Microsoft::Basix::Instrumentation::TraceManager::TraceMessage
solution: The issue can be temporarily mitigated by disabling tracing, which is done by adding the following value to the registry:  DisableEHUpload_<ClientVersion>  *The ClientVersion can be obtained in the "About" section of the Windows App.  For example:  reg.exe add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Terminal Server Client\Default" /v DisableEHUpload_1.2.6980.0 /t REG_DWORD /d 1 /f However, it only lasts until the app is updated to the next version.

symptom:
Azure Virtual Desktop (AVD) session hosts reported as unavailable Domain trust checks failed Multiple AVD safe URLs were unreachable AVD machines marked as unhealthy Event ID 3019 errors observed Error message observed:
“Transport received an exception Unable to connect to the remote server, when connecting to wss://rdbroker.wvd.microsoft.com//api/rdAgent/v1 for enum globalBrokerUri”   Internet access unavailable from affected machines during the issue window Issue observed across multiple host pools Approximately 200 users impacted
rootCause:
TCP port exhaustion events were identified on the affected AVD session hosts.
Port usage analysis showed the winres process consuming a high number of ports.
A third-party application (Produtivo) deployed via Intune was identified; after terminating the process and uninstalling the application, network connectivity was restored.
solution:
Uninstall the Produtivo application from all AVD session host machines. Remove the application deployment from Intune or any Group Policy that may be triggering reinstallation. Restart the affected AVD machines.  Note:
As this is a third-party application, further investigation or support must be engaged directly with the application vendor.

symptom: RDP shortpath traffic route validation. Customer has policies set so that the traffic does not leave his region. However he can see that RDP traffic to a specific IP, is still being routed to an address which is out of his region.
rootCause:
The data traffic in AVD
can either be a user traffic or a service traffic. User traffic is the data
accessed by the customer and this will very stay in the same region. However service
traffic is used by the VM to communicate with the back end control pane of
Azure infrastructure and this cannot be limited to a specific geo
solution:
Outbound traffic to IP
address in question is expected and legitimate.   Why This Traffic Occurs 
 The IP
     address belongs to Microsoft and is part of the new TURN relay range
     (51.xx.x.x/16) used by RDP Shortpath for public networks in
     Azure Virtual Desktop (AVD). When direct UDP connectivity cannot be
     established, Shortpath uses TURN relays to maintain session quality. This
     can result in multiple ephemeral UDP connections, which is normal and
     secure. 
 The
     traffic to the IP address in question aligns with Microsoft’s new Shortpath TURN relay
     design for AVD/Windows 365. It is Microsoft owned and part of ongoing
     service improvements to UDP media reliability. 
 High
     connection counts are normal when Shortpath is widely adopted; they
     reflect ephemeral UDP flows and keep alives, not data exfiltration.
     Customers can confirm via port 3478 UDP and TURN test outputs
